proof_stages.label: Algebraic optimization degrees as generic critical-point counts
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the roadmap and defines maximum-likelihood degree and linear-optimization degree as the generic number of complex critical points of algebraic objective functions on an algebraic variety.

proof_stages.label: Cotangent and conormal reformulation of critical points
proof_stages.summary: Critical points are reinterpreted as zeros of one-forms, so ML and LO degrees become intersection numbers or degrees of projections from the conormal variety in a cotangent-type bundle, with the singular case handled by taking the closure over the smooth locus.

proof_stages.label: Euler-characteristic formulas via index theory
proof_stages.summary: For smooth varieties the generic critical-point counts are identified, up to sign, with Euler characteristics: the ML degree with χ(X) in the torus case and the LO degree with χ(X minus a general affine hyperplane), explained via D-module/global index theorems or Morse-theoretic variants.

proof_stages.label: Singular correction by local Euler obstruction
proof_stages.summary: For singular varieties ordinary Euler characteristic no longer suffices, but replacing it by the weighted Euler characteristic determined by the local Euler obstruction restores the ML-degree formula.

proof_stages.label: Sectional ML degrees and conormal bidegrees
proof_stages.summary: The single ML degree is refined into sectional degrees from successive general hyperplane sections and into bidegrees of the conormal variety, and these two sequences are related by an explicit invertible linear transformation.

proof_stages.label: Chern-Mather class explanation of the ML transformations
proof_stages.summary: MacPherson’s Chern-class formalism, weighted by local Euler obstruction to give Chern-Mather classes, encodes the conormal bidegrees, while Aluffi’s hyperplane-section formula explains why the sectional and bidegree sequences determine one another.

proof_stages.label: Affine-linear analogue and logarithmic twisting
proof_stages.summary: The same refinement is developed for linear optimization in affine space, where sectional degrees and bidegrees coincide and their relation to Chern classes differs from the torus case because the relevant one-forms require a twist of the logarithmic cotangent bundle.

proof_stages.label: Reverse computations and nongeneric-parameter limits
proof_stages.summary: The topological formulas can be reversed to compute local Euler obstruction from ML-degree counts, and they also motivate studying how critical points collide or escape to infinity when the exponent or coefficient parameters become nongeneric.

examples.name: Closest-point/Euclidean-distance problem
examples.note: The closest-point problem motivates considering linear optimization degree, since after adding one coordinate the quadratic distance optimization can be viewed as a linear-function optimization on a lifted variety.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker concretely reduces the closest-point minimization problem by adding a variable and embedding in a higher affine space to turn it into a linear optimization problem.

passes.reason: The Euclidean-distance optimization problem is used as standard motivation for linear optimization degree, not as a structurally exceptional or adversarial case.

examples.name: The whole torus as the subvariety
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that for x=(C*)^n the conormal is just the zero section and the optimization count is trivial, clarifying what the conormal picture is not meant to depict.
examples.engagement_reason: For the whole torus, the speaker checks that the power functions have no critical points and identifies the conormal as the zero section C*^n × 0.

passes.reason: Taking the whole torus is the zero-constraint maximal subvariety with trivial conormal/zero critical points and full torus symmetry.

examples.name: Cuspidal curve in (C*)^2
examples.note: The plane cuspidal curve is used to show concretely how ordinary Euler characteristic gives the wrong ML degree while weighting the cusp by its local Euler obstruction gives the correct value.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes the cuspidal curve’s Euler characteristic after removing coordinate intersections, calculates the Euler obstruction at the cusp, and derives the weighted Euler characteristic/ML degree.

passes.reason: The cuspidal curve is a singular degeneration and is used to show the ordinary Euler-characteristic formula fails without Euler-obstruction weights.
